turns.content: I have a question about rose pruning, if you have a moment. I have rather let things get away from me in the garden and I think some of the bushes need attention.

Of course — now is actually a good time to be thinking about roses. April is right in the window for spring pruning in the UK, though ideally you'd have started a few weeks ago. What types of roses are we talking about, and how overgrown have they become?

There are quite a few. The garden was always my wife's domain, and she knew exactly what to do with each one. I have hybrid teas along the south wall, a rather sprawling rambler over the pergola, and several David Austin shrub roses in the main beds. I have been doing my best with them, but I should like to do it properly.

It's lovely that you're keeping the garden going. Each of those types does need a slightly different approach, so let me break it down:

**Hybrid teas** (the ones along the south wall):
- These benefit from fairly hard pruning in spring — cut them back to about 15-20cm from the ground
- Look for outward-facing buds and cut just above one at a 45-degree angle sloping away from the bud
- Remove any dead, damaged, or crossing stems entirely
- The goal is an open, vase-like shape that lets air circulate

**David Austin shrub roses:**
- These are more forgiving — they don't need to be cut as hard
- Reduce the overall height by about a third, and shape them into a rounded form
- Remove any dead wood and thin out congested growth in the centre
- They'll fill out beautifully through the summer

The **rambler** is a different matter — what time of year does it flower? Once in early summer, or repeatedly through the season?
It flowers once, in June. A tremendous flush of small pink blooms. It is rather magnificent when it performs, though it does try to consume the entire pergola if left unchecked.

That's a once-flowering rambler, and the important thing to know is: **don't prune it now**. Once-flowering ramblers bloom on last year's growth, so if you cut it back now, you'd be removing exactly the wood that's about to flower in June.

The right time to prune a once-flowering rambler is **immediately after it finishes blooming**, usually in July. At that point, you can:
- Cut out the oldest stems (the thick, woody ones) right down to the base — typically remove about a third of the oldest growth
- Tie in the new long shoots that emerged this year, as those will carry next year's flowers
- Trim back any side shoots to about two or three buds

For now, just tidy any dead bits and train any loose shoots so they're not flailing about. The big prune comes after the June show.

I am glad I asked before taking the secateurs to it. I very nearly did so last weekend. The hybrid teas I can manage, I think. Fifteen to twenty centimetres, outward-facing buds, open shape. The David Austins — a third off the top and thin the middle. Is that about right?

That's exactly right — you've got a very clear grasp of it. A few additional tips while you're at it:

- Use **sharp, clean secateurs** — a clean cut heals faster and reduces the risk of disease. If you're moving between bushes, a quick wipe of the blades with a cloth and some methylated spirit is good practice, especially if any of the roses have had blackspot
- **Remove any growth coming from below the graft union** on the hybrid teas — that's the knobbly bit near the base where the rose was grafted onto the rootstock. Any shoots from below that point are rootstock suckers and will be vigorous but won't produce the flowers you want
- After pruning, give them all a good feed — a handful of fish, blood and bone or a proprietary rose fertiliser worked into the soil around each bush, and then a thick mulch of well-rotted compost or bark

The mulch especially will help retain moisture through the summer and suppress weeds.

I have some well-rotted compost from the heap at the bottom of the garden. That should do nicely. And I have fish, blood and bone in the shed, I believe. One more question, if I may — a few of the David Austins have rather a lot of dead wood in the centre. Whole branches that are brown and dry. Is it sufficient to cut those out now, or should I be concerned about why they have died back?

It's good to cut those out now — dead wood in the centre is quite common, particularly after a cold winter or if the bushes are mature and haven't been thinned regularly. It doesn't necessarily indicate disease. When you cut into the dead wood, look at the cross-section: you want to cut back until you see **white or pale green pith** in the centre of the stem. If the pith is brown, keep cutting further down until you reach healthy wood.

If you notice any stems with dark, purplish-black lesions or cankers on the bark, that could be a sign of stem canker — cut those out well below the affected area and dispose of them in the bin rather than the compost heap. But straightforward dead wood after winter is nothing to worry about. Once you've cleared it out and the air can circulate through the centre of the bush, they should respond well.

Very good. I shall make a start on it this afternoon while the weather holds. It is rather satisfying work, pruning. One can see the results immediately. Thank you for the guidance — I feel rather more confident now than I did half an hour ago.

You're very welcome. It sounds like you have everything you need — sharp secateurs, compost, feed, and a fine afternoon. The roses will thank you for the attention, and you should see the results come June when everything is in bloom. If anything looks concerning once you get into the pruning, or if you want advice on the rambler when it's time to tackle that in July, feel free to ask. Enjoy the afternoon in the garden.
